title: FAWK: LLMs can write a language interpreter
author: todsacerdoti
summary:
After reading the book The AWK Programming Language
(recommended!), I was planning to try AWK
out on this year’s Advent of Code. Having some time off from work this week, I
tried to implement one of the problems
in it to get some practice, set up my tooling, see how hard AWK would be,
and… I found I’m FP-pilled.
I knew I’m addicted to the combination of algebraic data types (tagged unions)
and exhaustive pattern matching, but what got me this time was immutability,
lexical scope and the basic human right of being allowed to return arrays from
functions.
Part 1 of the Advent of Code problem was easy enough, but for part 2 (basically
a shortest path search with a twist, to not spoil too much), I found myself
unable to switch from my usual functional BFS
approach
to something mutable, and ended up trying to implement my functional approach in
AWK.
I was very lost by the time I had all this; I spent hours just solving what felt
like accidental complexity; things that I’d take for granted in more modern
languages.
Now, I know nobody said AWK is modern, or functional, or that it promises any
convenience for anything other than one-liners and basic scripts that fit under
a handful of lines. I don’t want to sound like I expect AWK to do any of this;
I knew I was stretching the tool when going in. But I couldn’t shake the feeling
that there’s a beautiful AWK-like language within reach, an iteration on the AWK
design (the pattern-action way of thinking is beautiful) that also gives us a
few of the things programming language designers have learnt over the 48 years
since AWK was born.
Stopping my attempts to solve the AoC puzzle in pure AWK, I wondered: what am I
missing here?
What if AWK had lexical scope instead of dynamic scope?
What if AWK had explicit globals, and everything else was local by default?
(This one, admittedly, might make programs a bit more verbose. I’m willing to
pay that cost.)
What if AWK had pipelines? (OK, now I’m reaching for syntax sugar…)
Now for the crazy, LLM-related part of the post. I didn’t want to spend days
implementing AWK from scratch or tweaking somebody else’s implementation. So I
tried to use Cursor Agent for a larger task than I usually do (I tend to ask
for very small targeted edits), and asked Sonnet 4.5 for a README with code
examples, and then a full
implementation in Python.
Note: I also asked for implementations in C, Haskell and Rust at the same
time, not knowing if any of the four would succeed, and they all seem to have
produced code that at least compiles/runs. I haven’t tried to test them or
even run them though. The PRs are
here.
I was very impressed—I still am! I expected the LLM to stumble and flail
around and ultimately get nothing done, but it did what I asked it for (gave me
an interpreter that could run those specific examples), and over the course
of a few chat sessions, I guided it towards implementing more and more of “the
rest of AWK”, together with an excessive amount of end-to-end tests.
The only time I could see it struggle was when I asked it to implement arbitrary
precision floating point operations without using an external library like
mpmath. It attempted to use Taylor series, but couldn’t get it right for at
least a few minutes. I chickened out and told it to uv add mpmath and simplify
the interpreter code. In a moment it was done.
Other things that I thought it would choke on, like print being both a
statement (with > and >> redirection support) and an expression, or
multi-dimensional arrays, or multi-line records, these were all implemented
correctly. Updating the test suite to also check for backwards compatibility
with GAWK - not an issue. Lexical scoping
and tricky closure environment behaviour - handled that just fine.
As the cool kids say, I have to update my priors. The frontier of what the
LLMs can do has moved since the last time I tried to vibe-code something. I
didn’t expect to have a working interpreter the same day I dreamt of a new
programming language. It now seems possible.
The downside of vibe coding the whole interpreter is that I have zero knowledge
of the code. I only interacted with the agent by telling it to implement a
thing and write tests for it, and I only really reviewed the tests. I reckon
this would be an issue in the future when I want to manually make some change
in the actual code, because I have no familiarity with it.
This also opened new questions for me wrt. my other projects where I’ve
previously run out of steam, eg. trying to implement a Hindley-Milner type
system for my
dream forever-WIP programming language Cara. It seems
I can now just ask the LLM to do it, and it will? But then, I don’t want to fall
into the trap where I am no longer able to work on the codebase myself. I want
to be familiar with and able to tinker on the code. I’d need to spend my time
reviewing and reading code instead of writing everything myself. Perhaps that’s
OK.
Performance of FAWK might be an issue as well, though right now it’s a non-goal,
given my intended use case is throwaway scripts for Advent of Code, nothing
user-facing.  And who knows, based on what I’ve seen, maybe I can instruct it to
rewrite it in Rust and have a decent chance of success?
For now, I’ll go dogfood my shiny new vibe-coded black box of a programming
language on the Advent of Code problem (and as many of the 2025 puzzles as I
can), and see what rough edges I can find. I expect them to be equal parts “not
implemented yet” and “unexpected interactions of new PL features with the old
ones”.
If you’re willing to jump through some Python project dependency hoops, you can
try to use FAWK too at your own risk, at Janiczek/fawk on
GitHub.
content:
# FAWK: LLMs Can Write a Language Interpreter  

*By todsacerdoti – Score: 69*  

---

## Why I Started with AWK

I was getting ready for this year’s **Advent of Code** and wanted to try solving the puzzles in **AWK** after rediscovering the classic book *The AWK Programming Language* (highly recommended!).  

- I wanted a quick setup to test the language.  
- I was curious how painful—or pleasant—AWK would be for a real problem.  

What I didn’t expect was that my love for **functional programming** would clash with AWK’s old‑school, line‑oriented design.

### The functional itch

- **Algebraic data types** (tagged unions) and exhaustive pattern matching feel natural to me.  
- **Immutability**, lexical scoping, and the ability to **return arrays from functions** are things I take for granted.  

When the Day 2 puzzle turned out to be a shortest‑path search with a twist, I tried to port my usual **BFS** (breadth‑first search) implementation into pure AWK.  The result? Hours of “accidental complexity”—details I never have to think about in languages like Haskell, Rust, or even modern Python.

---

## Imagining a Better AWK

While wrestling with the limitations, a few questions popped up:

- *What if AWK had lexical scope instead of dynamic scope?*  
- *What if every variable were local by default, with opt‑in globals?*  
- *What if AWK supported pipelines as first‑class syntax sugar?*  

I started to picture a language that kept AWK’s elegant **pattern‑action** mental model, but added the niceties that 48 years of language design have taught us.

---

## Enter the Large Language Model

I didn’t want to spend weeks re‑implementing AWK from scratch, so I turned to **Cursor Agent** and asked **Sonnet 4.5** to:

1. Write a README with usage examples.  
2. Produce a **full interpreter** in **Python** (and, just for fun, in C, Haskell, and Rust).  

> *Note:* I never ran the C/Haskell/Rust versions, but they all compiled.

### How it went

| Task | LLM behavior |
|------|--------------|
| Basic interpreter scaffolding | ✅ Delivered quickly, with clean code |
| Multi‑dimensional arrays & record handling | ✅ Implemented correctly |
| `print` as both statement and expression (with `>`/`>>` redirection) | ✅ Handled fine |
| Lexical scoping & closures | ✅ No issues |
| Arbitrary‑precision floating point **without** external libs | ❌ Struggled; tried a Taylor series, got stuck for minutes |
| Adding `mpmath` to simplify the fp implementation | ✅ Fixed in a flash |

The LLM kept producing runnable code, and with each short chat I nudged it to flesh out more of “the rest of AWK”.  I also added a **large test suite** to keep the engine honest. The result was a working interpreter—**FAWK** (Functional AWK)—in a single day.

---

## What I Learned

### 1. LLMs are farther up the chain than I thought  
The “frontier” of what an assistant can build has moved. Asking for a complete interpreter is no longer fantasy.

### 2. Knowledge gap is real  
Because I only **guided** the agent and inspected the tests, I have virtually zero mental model of the generated code. That could become a liability when I need to make manual changes.

### 3. Speed vs. Understanding trade‑off  
The speed is amazing, but the cost is the need to **read and review** the produced code afterwards—potentially turning a quick prototype into a heavyweight maintenance task.

### 4. Performance is a secondary concern  
FAWK is meant for throw‑away scripts (Advent of Code puzzles). It’s not tuned for speed, but that’s acceptable for its intended use.

### 5. “Vibe‑coding” opens new doors  
Projects that stalled for lack of time—like adding a **Hindley‑Milner type system** to my personal language *Cara*—might now be tackled by prompting an LLM. The challenge will be staying engaged with the codebase.

---

## Should You Play with FAWK?

If you’re comfortable with Python dependencies and don’t mind a “black‑box” interpreter, you can give FAWK a spin:

- Repository: **[github.com/Janiczek/fawk](https://github.com/Janiczek/fawk)**  
- Works for Advent of Code puzzles and any quick‑script tasks.  

Just remember: it’s a **research prototype**, not a production‑grade tool.

---

## Take‑away for the Rest of Us

- **LLMs can generate non‑trivial software** (interpreters, compilers, type checkers) when given clear goals and tests.  
- **Human oversight matters** – you still need to review, test, and understand the code you accept.  
- **Experimentation is cheap** – try prompting an LLM for a small component; you may be surprised at the results.

The line between “I wrote this code” and “the LLM wrote this code for me” is blurring. Embrace the help, but keep your own curiosity alive.

---

**Source:** [FAWK: LLMs can write a language interpreter – Hacker News](https://martin.janiczek.cz/2025/11/21/fawk-llms-can-write-a-language-interpreter.html)
